content.text: Every virtual machine running on AWS, every container orchestrated by ECS, and every serverless function executed by Lambda traces its roots to a single foundational technology: virtualization — the ability to run multiple isolated computing environments on a single physical machine.
What It Is
Virtualization is the process of creating a software-based (virtual) representation of physical computing resources — including CPU, memory, storage, and network interfaces. A software layer called a
hypervisor

(also called a Virtual Machine Monitor, or VMM) sits between the physical hardware and the virtual machines (VMs). It partitions the host's physical resources among multiple VMs, each of which behaves as a fully independent computer with its own operating system, file system, and network stack.

From the perspective of software running inside a VM, the virtual hardware is indistinguishable from physical hardware. The guest operating system believes it owns dedicated CPU cores, RAM, and a disk drive. The hypervisor enforces strict isolation between VMs — a crash or security breach in one VM does not affect others on the same host.

Virtualization predates cloud computing by decades — IBM pioneered it in the 1960s on mainframes to allow multiple users to share expensive hardware. Modern cloud computing is built almost entirely on top of this concept, extending it with automation, self-service provisioning, and global-scale orchestration systems.
Why It Matters

Without virtualization, cloud computing as we know it could not exist. The economic model of the cloud — pay-per-use, elastic scaling, multi-tenancy — depends entirely on the ability to carve a single physical server into dozens of isolated workspaces and rent each one independently.

Before virtualization became practical for commodity servers (roughly 2005–2007), utilization rates in enterprise data centers averaged 5–15%. A physical server was provisioned for one application, and the rest of its capacity sat idle. This was expensive and wasteful. Virtualization allowed a single server to run 10–40 isolated workloads simultaneously, dramatically improving utilization and reducing hardware costs.

For AWS specifically, understanding virtualization explains why EC2 instances have the performance characteristics they do, why certain instance types have dedicated hardware options, why the Nitro System matters, and how the shared responsibility model maps to hypervisor vs. guest OS boundaries. It also explains the difference between virtual machines and containers — a distinction critical for choosing the right compute service.
Benefits
Hardware consolidation.
Multiple workloads share physical resources, increasing utilization from single-digit percentages to 60–80% or higher in cloud environments.
Strong isolation.

Each VM runs in its own protected memory space. A faulty driver or kernel panic in one VM cannot crash another. In cloud multi-tenancy, this isolation separates different customers' workloads on the same physical host.
OS flexibility.
Different VMs on the same host can run entirely different operating systems — Linux, Windows, BSD — and different OS versions. This is impossible on bare metal without rebooting.
Snapshots and portability.

The entire state of a VM — memory, disk, CPU registers — can be captured in a snapshot (an Amazon Machine Image, or AMI, in AWS terms). VMs can be copied between hosts, restored to earlier states, or used as a template for launching new instances.
Live migration.

The hypervisor can move a running VM to a different physical host with minimal interruption (typically sub-second for memory copy phases). AWS uses this to perform hardware maintenance without forcing customers to stop their instances.
Rapid provisioning.
Launching a new VM from a pre-built image is a software operation that takes seconds to minutes, compared to weeks for physical server procurement and setup.
Trade-offs
Performance overhead.

A hypervisor adds a software layer between hardware and the OS. Even with hardware-assisted virtualization (Intel VT-x, AMD-V), there is some overhead for I/O, context switching, and privileged instructions. Modern hypervisors like Nitro have reduced this to near-zero for most workloads, but it is not literally zero.
Memory overhead.

Each VM runs a full operating system with its own kernel, libraries, and daemons. A minimal Linux VM might consume 200–500 MB of RAM even before the application starts. On a host with 256 GB RAM, this overhead limits how many small VMs can co-exist.
Boot time.

A VM must boot a full OS, which typically takes 30–90 seconds even with fast storage. Containers start in milliseconds by sharing the host kernel. For rapid scaling scenarios, this difference is significant.
Noisy neighbor.

Multiple VMs sharing a physical host compete for CPU cache, memory bandwidth, and I/O bandwidth. A VM performing heavy disk I/O can degrade the performance of co-located VMs. Cloud providers mitigate this through resource isolation mechanisms, but it cannot be completely eliminated on shared hardware.
Complexity.
Managing virtualized environments at scale requires orchestration tooling (vCenter, AWS EC2 Auto Scaling), monitoring at both the host and guest layers, and careful capacity planning.
How It Works
Hardware-Assisted Virtualization

Early virtualization relied entirely on software emulation — the hypervisor intercepted and translated privileged CPU instructions that guest OSes tried to execute. This was slow. In 2005–2006, Intel and AMD introduced CPU extensions (Intel VT-x and AMD-V respectively) that allow the hypervisor to run guest OS instructions directly on the CPU without translation, intervening only for genuinely privileged operations. This hardware-assisted virtualization (
HVM
) is now the dominant model and is what AWS EC2 uses exclusively.

HVM introduces a new CPU privilege ring structure. Traditional x86 processors have four privilege rings (0–3): the OS runs at ring 0 (most privileged) and applications at ring 3. With virtualization extensions, a new ring (-1, or VMX root mode on Intel) is added for the hypervisor. The guest OS continues to believe it runs at ring 0, but the CPU transparently traps certain operations and hands control to the hypervisor.
Memory Virtualization

Memory virtualization requires translating between three address spaces: the guest virtual address (what the application sees), the guest physical address (what the guest OS manages as if it owned physical RAM), and the host physical address (the real RAM location). Modern CPUs handle this with the Extended Page Tables (Intel EPT) or Nested Page Tables (AMD NPT) — hardware structures that let the CPU translate guest physical addresses to host physical addresses without hypervisor intervention on every memory access.
I/O Virtualization
I/O virtualization — network and storage access from VMs — is the most performance-sensitive area. Three approaches exist:
Emulated devices.

The hypervisor presents virtual devices (e.g., an emulated Intel E1000 NIC) that the guest OS's existing drivers can use. Highest compatibility, lowest performance — all I/O passes through the hypervisor.
Paravirtualized devices (virtio).

The guest OS uses a special driver that knows it is running in a VM and communicates directly with the hypervisor via a shared-memory ring buffer. Significantly faster than emulated devices; used in EC2's enhanced networking (ENA driver).
SR-IOV (Single Root I/O Virtualization).

A PCIe standard that allows a single physical NIC or storage controller to present multiple virtual functions (VFs) that are assigned directly to VMs. The VM's driver communicates with the hardware directly, bypassing the hypervisor for data-plane operations. Used in EC2 Enhanced Networking and EBS optimized instances.
Architecture: Type 1 vs Type 2 Hypervisors
Hypervisors come in two fundamental architectures. The diagram below illustrates how each model stacks its layers:

attrs.alt: Type 1 bare-metal hypervisor vs Type 2 hosted hypervisor architecture comparison

content.text: Type 1 — Bare-Metal Hypervisor

A Type 1 hypervisor runs directly on the physical hardware — there is no host operating system beneath it. The hypervisor itself is the OS for the machine. Examples include VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, Xen (used in early AWS EC2), and the AWS Nitro Hypervisor.

Because there is no host OS consuming CPU and memory, Type 1 hypervisors have lower overhead and better isolation. They are used exclusively in production cloud environments. AWS's transition from Xen to the Nitro Hypervisor further reduced overhead: Nitro offloads network and storage I/O to dedicated Nitro Cards (custom ASICs), so the hypervisor itself has an almost negligible footprint — the vast majority of the host's CPU and RAM is available to customer VMs.
Type 2 — Hosted Hypervisor

A Type 2 hypervisor runs as an application on top of a conventional host operating system. Examples include Oracle VirtualBox, VMware Workstation, and QEMU (in user-mode). VMs launched inside a Type 2 hypervisor make system calls that go through the host OS, adding latency. Type 2 hypervisors are used for development, testing, and local experimentation — not in production cloud environments.
The AWS Nitro System
AWS introduced the Nitro System starting in 2017 as a ground-up redesign of EC2's virtualization infrastructure. It has three main components:
Nitro Cards.

Purpose-built hardware cards that handle network I/O, EBS storage I/O, and instance monitoring. Offloading I/O from the host CPU to dedicated silicon means I/O operations no longer compete with customer workloads for CPU cycles.
Nitro Hypervisor.

A lightweight hypervisor that handles memory and CPU virtualization. Because I/O is offloaded to Nitro Cards, the Nitro Hypervisor itself has a very small footprint and minimal CPU overhead, unlike traditional hypervisors that also manage I/O.
Nitro Security Chip.
A hardware root of trust that secures the boot process, continuously validates firmware, and provides the hardware-enforced isolation that underlies the shared responsibility model.

The Nitro System enables capabilities impossible on the previous Xen-based infrastructure: bare-metal instances (where no hypervisor sits between customer workloads and the CPU — useful for running Xen or KVM inside EC2 for nested virtualization), the highest-memory instances (up to 24 TB RAM), and the highest-performance networking (up to 400 Gbps on some instance types).
When to Use Virtualization
For AWS workloads, the question is less "should I use virtualization" (the answer is almost always yes — EC2 uses it by default) and more "which compute model fits my workload":
EC2 instances (VMs):
Use when you need a full OS, persistent processes, long-running workloads, custom kernel configurations, or workloads that run a hypervisor themselves (nested virtualization).
Containers (ECS/EKS):
Use when multiple isolated application processes can share a kernel — faster startup, higher density, more efficient use of EC2 instances underneath.
Bare-metal instances (e.g. i3.metal, c5.metal):
Use when the workload requires direct hardware access, custom kernel modules, performance tuning that eliminates any hypervisor overhead, or licensing tied to physical sockets.
Serverless (Lambda):

Use when per-request billing, zero idle cost, and automatic scaling are priorities. Lambda runs on Firecracker — a lightweight microVM hypervisor purpose-built for serverless workloads — achieving sub-100ms startup times with strong isolation.
How to Implement: Launching and Inspecting a VM on AWS

On AWS, EC2 handles all hypervisor-level details automatically. As a user, you interact with VMs through the EC2 API. The steps below demonstrate provisioning an EC2 instance using the CLI and inspecting its virtualization type.
Step 1: Check an AMI's virtualization type
AMIs specify whether instances launched from them use HVM (hardware virtual machine) or paravirtual (PV) virtualization. Modern EC2 instances exclusively use HVM; PV is legacy.

# Query an AMI for its virtualization type
aws ec2 describe-images \
  --image-ids ami-0abcdef1234567890 \
  --query 'Images[0].{Name:Name,VirtType:VirtualizationType,HypervisorType:Hypervisor,RootDevType:RootDeviceType}' \
  --output table

# Expected output:
# ------------------------------------
# | DescribeImages                   |
# +-------------+--------------------+
# | HypervisorType | xen            |
# | Name          | amzn2-ami-hvm   |
# | RootDevType   | ebs             |
# | VirtType      | hvm             |
# +-------------+--------------------+

# Note: AWS reports HypervisorType as 'xen' for historical reasons, 
# but Nitro instances actually run the Nitro Hypervisor internally.
Step 2: Confirm if an instance uses Nitro

# Describe a running instance to check hypervisor info
aws ec2 describe-instances \
  --instance-ids i-0123456789abcdef0 \
  --query 'Reservations[0].Instances[0].{Type:InstanceType,Hypervisor:Hypervisor,EnaSupport:EnaSupport,SriovNetSupport:SriovNetSupport}' \
  --output table

# From inside a running instance, detect Nitro via the device names:
# Nitro instances use NVMe device names (/dev/nvme0n1) instead of /dev/xvda
lsblk

# Check the hypervisor brand from inside the guest
dmesg | grep -i 'hypervisor\|xen\|nitro\|kvm'

# On Nitro instances you'll see:
# [    0.000000] Hypervisor detected: KVM
# (Nitro presents itself as KVM to guest OSes)
Step 3: List instance types by hypervisor support

# Find all instance types that support Nitro (indicated by bare-metal support
# or NVMe/ENA support as proxy signals)
aws ec2 describe-instance-types \
  --filters 'Name=supported-features,Values=nitro-enclaves' \
  --query 'InstanceTypes[].InstanceType' \
  --output text | tr '\t' '\n' | sort | head -20

# Or check if a specific type is Nitro-based:
aws ec2 describe-instance-types \
  --instance-types m5.xlarge \
  --query 'InstanceTypes[0].{Hypervisor:Hypervisor,BareMetal:BareMetal,NVMeSupport:InstanceStorageInfo.NvmeSupport}'
Examples
Example 1: Comparing HVM vs PV AMIs (Legacy Reference)
Modern AWS usage is exclusively HVM. The following shows how to filter AMIs by virtualization type — useful if you encounter legacy PV AMIs in an older account and need to migrate to HVM.

# List only HVM AMIs (what all modern instances use)
aws ec2 describe-images \
  --owners amazon \
  --filters \
    'Name=virtualization-type,Values=hvm' \
    'Name=name,Values=amzn2-ami-hvm-*-x86_64-gp2' \
    'Name=state,Values=available' \
  --query 'sort_by(Images, &CreationDate)[-3:].{ID:ImageId,Name:Name,VirtType:VirtualizationType}' \
  --output table

# List legacy PV AMIs (for reference - avoid using these)
aws ec2 describe-images \
  --owners amazon \
  --filters \
    'Name=virtualization-type,Values=paravirtual' \
    'Name=state,Values=available' \
  --query 'Images[:5].{ID:ImageId,Name:Name,VirtType:VirtualizationType}' \
  --output table
Example 2: Creating an AMI (VM Snapshot)

One of the most powerful virtualization capabilities is creating a snapshot of a running VM's disk — an AMI — and using it to launch identical instances. This is the basis of immutable infrastructure patterns in AWS.

# Create an AMI from a running EC2 instance
# This captures the root EBS volume as a snapshot and registers it as an AMI
aws ec2 create-image \
  --instance-id i-0123456789abcdef0 \
  --name "my-app-server-$(date +%Y%m%d)" \
  --description "Production app server image after v2.3.1 deploy" \
  --no-reboot \
  --tag-specifications 'ResourceType=image,Tags=[{Key=Environment,Value=production},{Key=Version,Value=2.3.1}]'

# Check AMI creation status (takes a few minutes)
aws ec2 describe-images \
  --owners self \
  --filters 'Name=name,Values=my-app-server-*' \
  --query 'Images[*].{ID:ImageId,Name:Name,State:State,Created:CreationDate}' \
  --output table

# Once 'available', launch a new instance from this AMI
aws ec2 run-instances \
  --image-id ami-<new-ami-id> \
  --instance-type m5.large \
  --count 3
Real-World Example

A software company runs a CI/CD pipeline that builds and tests code on every pull request. Each test run needs an isolated, clean environment to avoid interference between concurrent test suites. On on-premises infrastructure, this meant maintaining a pool of dedicated test servers that sat idle between builds and became configuration drift risks over time.

With AWS virtualization, each CI job launches a fresh EC2 instance from a pre-baked AMI (containing all build tools, language runtimes, and test dependencies), runs the test suite, then terminates the instance. Because AMIs are VM snapshots, every build starts from an identical, known-good state. Parallel builds run on separate instances — true OS-level isolation, not just process isolation. The company pays only for the minutes each test run uses, rather than maintaining idle capacity.

This pattern — ephemeral, snapshot-based VMs — is also the basis of AWS CodeBuild (which uses a similar model internally) and is why AMI management (keeping images updated, tagged, and rotated) is a key operational concern for teams running significant EC2 workloads.
Common Mistakes
Choosing the wrong virtualization-based instance type

Selecting a previous-generation instance type (e.g., m3, c3, t1) to save money means using older Xen-based virtualization with higher overhead, older network performance, and no support for enhanced networking (ENA/SR-IOV). Modern instance families (m5, c5, r5, t3 and newer) use Nitro and deliver significantly better performance per dollar.
Confusing VMs with containers

Containers (Docker, ECS) are often described as "lightweight VMs," but this is inaccurate and leads to architectural mistakes. Containers share the host kernel — there is no separate OS per container, no hypervisor, and therefore no hardware-level isolation between containers on the same host. VMs provide stronger isolation (separate kernels, hardware-enforced memory boundaries). Using containers where VM-level isolation is required (e.g., for multi-tenant SaaS with sensitive data) can be a security design error.
Not using ENA drivers

Nitro instances deliver network bandwidth through the Elastic Network Adapter (ENA), which requires ENA drivers in the guest OS. Modern Amazon Linux, Amazon Linux 2, and Amazon Linux 2023 AMIs include ENA drivers by default. However, older custom AMIs or imported VM images may not — resulting in degraded network performance that appears as an application bottleneck but is actually a missing driver.

# Verify ENA driver is loaded on a running instance
modinfo ena
# Should return driver info including version

# If not present, install it (Amazon Linux 2)
sudo yum install -y kernel-devel-$(uname -r)
git clone https://github.com/amzn/amzn-drivers.git
cd amzn-drivers/kernel/linux/ena
make && sudo make install

# Check that the ENI uses ENA
aws ec2 describe-instances \
  --instance-ids i-0123456789abcdef0 \
  --query 'Reservations[0].Instances[0].EnaSupport'
Using PV AMIs on HVM instance types

Paravirtual AMIs cannot be launched on HVM-only instance types (all current-generation instances). Attempting to do so results in an "InvalidAMIVirtualizationType" error. The fix is to migrate the workload to an HVM AMI, which is straightforward for most Linux distributions.
Best Practices
Always use current-generation Nitro instance families.

m5, c5, r5, t3, and their descendants (m6i, c6i, r6g, etc.) run on Nitro and deliver better performance, lower overhead, higher network throughput, and access to features like NVMe storage and 400 Gbps networking.
Build and maintain golden AMIs.

A golden AMI is a pre-hardened, pre-configured base image with all agents (SSM, CloudWatch, security scanning), kernel updates, and required software installed. Launching instances from golden AMIs eliminates configuration drift and reduces boot time compared to bootstrapping from a bare AMI.
Use EC2 Placement Groups for latency-sensitive clusters.

Cluster placement groups ask AWS to place instances on the same underlying hardware rack or spine, minimizing inter-instance network latency. Useful for HPC workloads, distributed databases, or any workload where VM-to-VM communication latency matters.
Prefer Dedicated Hosts for licensing compliance.

Some software licenses (Windows Server, SQL Server, Oracle) are tied to physical sockets. Dedicated Hosts give you a physical host allocated exclusively to your account, enabling BYOL compliance while still using EC2 virtualization.
Enable Instance Metadata Service v2 (IMDSv2).
IMDSv2 uses session-oriented requests, protecting against SSRF attacks that could steal IAM credentials from the instance metadata endpoint. Require IMDSv2 at launch with the
--metadata-options HttpTokens=required
flag.
Performance and Scalability

The overhead of hardware-assisted virtualization on Nitro is measurably small. AWS publishes benchmarks showing Nitro instances achieving performance within 1–3% of bare metal for CPU-intensive workloads. For network and storage I/O, Nitro's hardware offload means virtualized instances often outperform bare-metal systems running non-optimized I/O stacks, because the Nitro Cards handle I/O without consuming host CPU.

Network performance scales with instance size: a t3.micro gets up to 5 Gbps burst bandwidth; a c5n.18xlarge gets 100 Gbps guaranteed. For latency-sensitive workloads, Nitro instances support Enhanced Networking (ENA), which delivers consistent sub-100 microsecond latency between instances in the same AZ — critical for distributed databases and real-time messaging systems.

Memory bandwidth is a key constraint for in-memory databases and analytics workloads. EC2's high-memory instance families (r-series, x-series) are optimized for this, with x2iedn instances offering up to 3 TB of memory per instance. For workloads that need to spread across multiple large VM instances, the Nitro architecture ensures each VM gets predictable, dedicated memory bandwidth.
Tooling and Ecosystem
EC2 Image Builder.

AWS-managed service for automating the creation, testing, and distribution of AMIs. Define a recipe (base AMI + components + tests), and Image Builder produces a hardened, tested AMI on a schedule or on demand.
AWS VM Import/Export.
Imports existing on-premises VM disk images (VMware VMDK, VHD, OVA) as AMIs, enabling migration of on-premises VMs to EC2 without reconfiguring the OS.
Firecracker.

An open-source microVM hypervisor developed by AWS, written in Rust. Firecracker is used internally by Lambda and Fargate to run thousands of customer functions with sub-100ms cold starts and strong VM-level isolation. It is also available as an open-source tool for building your own serverless platforms.
AWS Nitro Enclaves.

An EC2 feature that creates isolated, CPU and memory-partitioned environments within an EC2 instance, with no persistent storage, no interactive access, and no external networking. Used for processing highly sensitive data (keys, certificates, PII) in a hardened environment.
Packer (HashiCorp).

An open-source tool for building machine images across multiple platforms. Packer can build AMIs from configuration scripts or Ansible playbooks, making AMI creation reproducible and version-controlled.

Security and Reliability
VM Isolation and the Hypervisor Attack Surface

The hypervisor is a security boundary — it must prevent one VM from reading or writing another VM's memory, accessing another VM's I/O channels, or influencing the hypervisor itself. A hypervisor vulnerability that allows a "VM escape" (code executing in one VM breaking out to affect the hypervisor or another VM) is one of the most serious security issues in cloud computing. This is why AWS invests heavily in reducing the Nitro Hypervisor's attack surface: the hypervisor has a very small codebase with a limited number of system calls, reducing the opportunity for exploitable bugs.

Spectre and Meltdown (discovered in 2018) demonstrated that hardware microarchitecture vulnerabilities could allow cross-VM data leakage even without a hypervisor bug. AWS responded by enabling microarchitectural mitigations (kernel page-table isolation, retpoline) at the host level and offering customers the option to use Dedicated Hosts or Dedicated Instances to avoid sharing physical hardware with other customers.
Reliability and Live Migration

When AWS needs to perform maintenance on a physical host — hardware replacement, hypervisor updates, security patches — it uses live migration to move running VMs to different hosts without interruption. This is possible because the VM's memory state is continuously copied to the destination host while the VM runs, with a final brief pause (typically under 1 second) when the remaining dirty memory pages are synchronized.

AWS sends advance notice of scheduled maintenance through AWS Health and the EC2 console. For most instance types, customers can also initiate instance migration themselves using the "Stop and Start" workflow — stopping an instance causes it to be restarted on a different physical host, which can resolve transient hardware degradation.
Mental Model

Think of a hypervisor like the operating system's job scheduler, but for entire computers instead of individual programs. Just as a single-core CPU gives each running program a time slice and an illusion of exclusive access, a hypervisor gives each VM a time slice of physical CPU and an illusion of exclusive hardware — its own memory, its own disk, its own network card.

The analogy extends to isolation: programs on a multitasking OS share the CPU but are protected from reading each other's memory by the OS. VMs are more strongly isolated — each runs a full OS that further separates applications within that VM, and the hypervisor hardware protects VMs from each other at an even lower level.

When AWS launches an EC2 instance for you, it is doing what an OS does when you start a new process — but at the scale of an entire computer. The Nitro Hypervisor is the scheduler; your EC2 instance is the process; and the physical host is the CPU. Everything else in AWS cloud computing — Auto Scaling, AMIs, Placement Groups, EBS volumes — is infrastructure built on top of this foundational layer.
Key Takeaways
Virtualization runs multiple isolated VMs on a single physical host using a hypervisor, enabling the multi-tenancy and elastic provisioning that make cloud computing economically viable.

Type 1 (bare-metal) hypervisors run directly on hardware without a host OS and are used in all production cloud environments; Type 2 hypervisors run on top of a host OS and are used for development and local testing.
AWS's Nitro System offloads I/O to dedicated hardware (Nitro Cards), leaving near-full host CPU and memory available for customer workloads and reducing the hypervisor's attack surface.
Hardware-assisted virtualization (Intel VT-x / AMD-V) enables guest OSes to run most instructions at native speed, with the CPU trapping only genuinely privileged operations to the hypervisor.

VMs provide OS-level isolation (separate kernels); containers provide process-level isolation (shared kernel). Choosing between them depends on the required isolation boundary and acceptable startup latency.
Always use current-generation Nitro-based EC2 instance families; older Xen-based families have higher I/O overhead, lower network performance, and lack features like NVMe storage and ENA networking.
AMIs are VM snapshots — the foundational primitive for immutable infrastructure, golden images, and rapid, identical instance provisioning at scale.
